findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `tests/unit/plan_review/` should be created as a new subdirectory (and update CI config if needed), or update all test-file paths to the flat `tests/unit/test_det_citation.py` / `tests/unit/test_citation_rubric_contract.py` pattern that all existing plan-review tests follow.
evidence: Plan AC: `pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review/test_det_citation.py::test_parse`

Actual directory listing of tests/unit/ shows no plan_review/ subdirectory; all existing plan-review tests (test_det_floor_operator_attested.py, test_plan_review.py, test_pass1_recall.py, etc.) are flat under tests/unit/.
findings.finding: The plan names test paths under `tests/unit/plan_review/` (e.g. `tests/unit/plan_review/test_det_citation.py`, `tests/unit/plan_review/test_citation_rubric_contract.py`), but this subdirectory does not exist — all plan-review unit tests live flat in `tests/unit/` (e.g. `test_det_floor_operator_attested.py`, `test_plan_review.py`). The proving commands in every AC will fail with a collection error unless this is addressed.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria (proving commands) and Files likely touched — test file paths
scenarios: All five pytest proving commands in the ACs silently fail with 'no such file or directory' or pytest collection error, making every AC unverifiable as written.
A developer creating the test files at the named path also creates an unregistered test directory that may not be picked up by CI pytest config.
findings.suggested_fix: Change all `tests/unit/plan_review/` path references in the ACs and Files likely touched to `tests/unit/test_det_citation.py` and `tests/unit/test_citation_rubric_contract.py` to match the established flat layout.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or ground the `accruing_since` constant claim: grep confirms no `det_*` or `plan_review/` module uses this field; either drop the reference entirely or cite the actual existing pattern that `det_citation.py` should follow (e.g., the coverage-dict approach used by all P1–P9 checks).
evidence: Grep of all plan_review/ and llm/ for 'accruing_since' returns zero hits — the constant does not exist in any det_* or plan_review module.

accruing_since is defined and used exclusively in rebar/metrics/registry.py:62, rebar/metrics/event_metrics.py:351, rebar/metrics/sidecar_metrics.py:273, rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py:260 — a different module family entirely.
det_operator_attested.py (the stated pattern model) has NO accruing_since constant — it is a pure stdlib-re leaf with no such field.
Adopting accruing_since in det_citation.py would introduce a metrics-registry concept into the DET floor with no existing usage pattern there.
findings.finding: The plan's `accruing_since` module-level constant is cargo-culted from the wrong module family (`rebar.metrics.registry`) and has zero precedent in any DET plan-review module.
findings.location: Layer 1 / det_citation.py description: 'accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules'
scenarios: Implementer searches for 'accruing_since' in the plan_review/ package, finds nothing, and is confused about what 'matching the existing reader modules' means.
Implementer copies the pattern from rebar.metrics.registry, adding an unnecessary field that det_floor never reads or surfaces.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `accruing_since` sentence. The existing DET advisory convention is to record state in the `coverage` dict of the returned `DetResult` (see `p6_ac_quality`'s `cov` dict). If a timestamp is wanted, document it as a plain dict key in `DetResult.coverage`, not an `accruing_since` module-level constant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify or remove the `accruing_since` module-level constant requirement: no existing `plan_review/` module uses this pattern (it lives only in `src/rebar/metrics/`), so either name the actual reader module that uses it, describe the infrastructure that reads it, or drop the requirement.
evidence: Plan text: 'accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules'
Search for 'accruing_since' in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/: zero results
Search for '_ACCRUING_SINCE' in full repo: only src/rebar/metrics/event_metrics.py:313, src/rebar/metrics/sidecar_metrics.py:252, src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py:236
ABSENCE: no plan_review module uses this pattern — no 'reader module' in the target subsystem has accruing_since
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `det_citation.py` should carry an `accruing_since` module-level constant 'matching the existing reader modules', implying this is a convention in the plan_review subsystem. A search of the entire codebase finds `_ACCRUING_SINCE` only in `src/rebar/metrics/` (event_metrics.py, sidecar_metrics.py, git_metrics.py) — zero occurrences in any `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` module. The convention does not exist in the named subsystem, making the assertion unverifiable and the implementation guidance potentially misleading.
findings.location: Layer 1 — deterministic, ADVISORY section: 'accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules'
scenarios: An implementer adds `accruing_since` to `det_citation.py` trying to mimic an existing convention but no other plan_review module uses it, leading to a gratuitous inconsistency.
A reviewer expects `accruing_since` to carry observable semantics (e.g., metric accrual gating) in the plan_review lane, but no infrastructure in that lane reads it, making it dead code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either remove the `accruing_since` claim (if it is not a real pattern) or identify the actual module that uses it and cite that module+line so the implementor has a grounded precedent.
evidence: Search of det_operator_attested.py returns no `accruing_since` symbol.
Search of entire src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ for `accruing_since` returns no matches.
The plan says this 'mirrors det_operator_attested' but det_operator_attested has no such constant.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `accruing_since` is a module-level constant that `det_citation.py` should carry, claiming it 'matches the existing reader modules' — but `det_operator_attested.py` (the stated precedent module) has no `accruing_since` constant, and a search of the plan_review package finds no such attribute. This is a hallucinated implementation detail that will require the implementor to invent the semantics from scratch.
findings.location: Layer 1 description: '`accruing_since` for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules'
scenarios: Implementor either omits the constant (silent behavior divergence) or must reverse-engineer what it should contain with no grounded precedent, risking a misaligned implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'accruing_since … matching the existing reader modules' clause in Layer 1 with either (a) the actual field/pattern det_citation should emit (e.g., a plain timestamp constant in the DetResult coverage dict), or (b) remove the clause if no such field is needed in the DET advisory lane.
evidence: det_operator_attested.py has no accruing_since field — the module contains only ac_item_lines(), operator_evidence_ac_gaps(), operator_evidence_issues(), and two re.Pattern constants
accruing_since appears exclusively in the metrics subsystem: rebar/metrics/registry.py:62 (MetricSpec dataclass field), rebar/metrics/event_metrics.py:351, rebar/metrics/sidecar_metrics.py:273
DetResult dataclass (det_floor.py:81-96) has no accruing_since field; det_advisory_findings() emits dicts with finding/evidence/impact/suggested_fix/criteria/tier keys only
findings.finding: The plan references 'accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules' as if det_operator_attested (the claimed analog) carries an accruing_since field — but that module is pure stdlib re with no such field, and accruing_since belongs to the rebar.metrics.registry subsystem (rebar/metrics/registry.py:62), not the DET advisory lane. An implementer following this spec will find no accruing_since pattern in the analog module and no DET advisory structure that accepts this field, leaving the requirement unimplementable as written.
findings.location: Layer 1 — deterministic, ADVISORY section: 'accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules'
scenarios: Implementer looks for accruing_since in det_operator_attested.py (the claimed analog), finds nothing, then searches the plan_review package and still finds nothing — the requirement is ungroundable
Implementer adds a spurious accruing_since field to DetResult or the emitted advisory dict, which is then silently ignored or causes a schema mismatch
findings.suggested_fix: If a freshness marker is needed, define it as a plain string constant (e.g., `CITATION_LINT_SINCE = "<ISO date>"`) stored in the coverage dict under a named key, mirroring how det_floor.py coverage dicts work — not an accruing_since metric-registry pattern that doesn't exist in this subsystem.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify or remove the `accruing_since` module-level constant claim: confirm whether det_citation.py actually needs this field (it has no consumers in the plan_review layer), or remove/replace the assertion with the actual convention det_ advisory modules use (none — det_floor.DetResult carries no such timestamp).
evidence: Search for 'accruing_since' in src/rebar/llm returns zero hits
det_operator_attested.py has no accruing_since constant — it is stdlib-only (re module)
det_floor.py has no accruing_since constant
accruing_since only appears in src/rebar/metrics/ (registry.py, event_metrics.py, sidecar_metrics.py, git_metrics.py) — a completely separate subsystem
The plan asserts this is an existing convention in the plan_review det_ modules, but no such convention is present
findings.finding: The plan asserts that det_citation.py will carry a module-level `accruing_since` constant 'matching the existing reader modules,' implying this is an established convention in plan_review det_ modules — but no such constant exists in det_floor.py, det_operator_attested.py, or any other plan_review module. The `accruing_since` field belongs to the rebar.metrics registry, not the det_ advisory layer.
findings.location: Layer 1 section: 'accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules'
scenarios: Implementer follows the spec and introduces accruing_since into det_citation.py, creating an inconsistency with every other plan_review module that has no such field
Implementer searches for the convention in sibling modules, finds nothing, and is left with an underspecified implementation detail
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `accruing_since` sentence entirely from Layer 1, or replace it with a concrete statement of what det_citation's DetResult will carry (e.g., 'det_citation returns DetResult objects with id="P-CIT" and blocking=False, consistent with the advisory lane contract already defined by DetResult in det_floor.py').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify what `accruing_since` means in the DET-signal context: either remove it (DET signals don't carry this field — see `DetResult` dataclass), point to the specific DET-tier pattern it should mirror, or confirm it is a new field being added to `DetResult`.
evidence: Search for 'accruing_since' in src/rebar/llm returns zero hits
accruing_since appears only in src/rebar/metrics/ (registry.py:62, event_metrics.py:351, sidecar_metrics.py:273, git_metrics.py:260)
det_operator_attested.py has no accruing_since constant; it is a pure DET leaf module with stdlib re only
det_floor.py DetResult dataclass has no accruing_since field
findings.finding: The `accruing_since` module-level constant called for in det_citation has no precedent or definition in the DET tier: searching the codebase shows `accruing_since` is exclusively a metrics-registry concept (src/rebar/metrics/registry.py, event_metrics.py) and is absent from every DET module (det_floor.py, det_operator_attested.py, det_lint.py). No DET signal carries this field. The plan directs the implementer to match 'the existing reader modules', but no reader module (DET tier) has this constant — they are in the metrics tier under a different interface contract.
findings.location: Layer 1 prose: 'accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules'
scenarios: Implementer searches for the 'accruing_since' pattern in DET modules and finds nothing — either copies the wrong metrics-tier interface or invents something inconsistent with both tiers.
The emitted det_citation advisory signal silently omits or misnames the field, breaking any consumer that aggregates DET coverage.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `accruing_since` reference or replace it with the concrete `DetResult` field the new check should populate (e.g., `coverage={'ran': True, ...}` pattern used by all existing DET checks). If a date stamp is genuinely needed, name it explicitly as a new `DetResult` field and add it to the dataclass spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit edge-verification step to the Layer-2 PROCEDURE (e.g., 'before checking coverage, verify via show_ticket(C) that C.deps contains {blocks→P} OR P.deps contains {depends_on→C}; if not, skip coverage check and let the finding stand') and ensure test_citation_rubric_contract verifies the rubric includes that step, not just the coverage-check protocol.
evidence: "TRIGGER: a grounding finder (G1G2/E4/E6) is about to flag a symbol/functionality as missing, and that symbol is cited <subject> [rebar:<C>] with an edge VERIFIED by Layer 1."
"PROCEDURE: the finder retrieves C via the show_ticket(C) tool and judges whether C's plan/file_impact establishes the SPECIFIC functionality" — no edge-check step listed
"Anti-bypass WITHOUT a new floor block: crediting requires BOTH a Layer-1-verified edge AND affirmative LLM coverage"
AC: "Rubrics G1G2/E4/E6 carry the [rebar:<id>] rule with the LLM show_ticket coverage PROTOCOL" — does not explicitly require the rubric to include edge-verification instructions
findings.finding: The Layer-2 trigger says "edge VERIFIED by Layer 1" but LLM reviewers run independently of det_floor's Layer-1 output; the Layer-2 PROCEDURE lists only a coverage check (show_ticket + coverage judgment) and never specifies an independent edge-verification step. The anti-bypass paragraph then asserts 'crediting requires BOTH a Layer-1-verified edge AND affirmative LLM coverage' — yet the only specified mechanism for Layer 2 to know the edge is verified is absent from the PROCEDURE. An implementer writing the rubric from the PROCEDURE spec would produce a rubric that checks coverage only, satisfying test_citation_rubric_contract while leaving the anti-bypass edge-check unenforced at runtime.
findings.location: Approach & rationale → Layer 2 PROCEDURE + TRIGGER; Approach & rationale → Anti-bypass paragraph; AC: Rubrics G1G2/E4/E6 carry the protocol
scenarios: An implementer writes the rubric to call show_ticket and check coverage only; test_citation_rubric_contract passes (protocol text present); but at runtime the LLM drops a missing-symbol finding for a fabricated citation with no real depends_on edge
A reviewer reads Layer 2's PROCEDURE and cannot determine whether the LLM must re-verify the edge itself or trust that Layer 1 already filtered — two incompatible implementations are possible
findings.suggested_fix: In the Layer-2 PROCEDURE, prepend a step: '(0) EDGE CHECK: call show_ticket on the plan ticket P and on C; confirm P.deps contains {depends_on→C} OR C.deps contains {blocks→P}. If neither, do NOT credit the citation — proceed as if no [rebar:] tag is present.' Then add a rubric-contract assertion in test_citation_rubric_contract that checks for the presence of edge-verification language (e.g., grep for 'depends_on' or 'edge' in the updated rubric files).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the Layer-1 edge-verification result is surfaced to the LLM agent running G1G2/E4/E6 — e.g., injected into the rubric call context as a pre-computed `verified_citations` list, or by specifying that the agent independently re-checks the edge via `show_ticket` (and document this as the agent's own verification step, not a dependency on Layer-1 output).
evidence: Layer 1 is det_citation.py in the DET Python tier (run_det_floor → advisory lane); Layer 2 is rubric text in plan_review_G1G2.md / E4.md / E6.md (LLM agent tier)
The LLM agent has show_ticket and filesystem tools (src/rebar/llm/pai_tools.py) but cannot call Python functions from det_citation
The plan's AC for rubric contract (test_citation_rubric_contract.py) checks only text-presence of the protocol, not the delivery mechanism
det_floor run results are returned as DetResult objects; no existing path surfaces DET advisory text into LLM rubric context (confirmed by searching pass1.py)
findings.finding: The Layer-1 → Layer-2 signal pathway is unspecified: the plan says the Layer-2 TRIGGER fires when 'a grounding finder (G1G2/E4/E6) is about to flag a symbol and that symbol is cited `[rebar:<C>]` with an edge VERIFIED by Layer 1' — but Layer 1 runs deterministically inside `det_floor.py` (Python, no LLM), while Layer 2 runs inside an LLM agent that only has the `show_ticket` tool. No mechanism is specified for how the agent learns which citations have been Layer-1 verified, how the Layer-1 output is surfaced to the rubric context, or whether the agent is expected to re-check edges itself using show_ticket (which would make Layer 1 redundant for the LLM path).
findings.location: Layer 2 prose: 'TRIGGER: a grounding finder (G1G2/E4/E6) is about to flag a symbol...and that symbol is cited [rebar:<C>] with an edge VERIFIED by Layer 1'
scenarios: Implementer writes the rubric text with 'edge VERIFIED by Layer 1' but the LLM agent receiving this rubric has no structured input saying which citations were verified — the condition is untestable at runtime.
Agent re-implements edge-checking with show_ticket (making Layer 1 redundant for the LLM path), or ignores the Layer-1 gate entirely and credits all self-stated citations.

The DET advisory finding from Layer 1 (unbacked citation) and the LLM TRIGGER (edge not verified) are evaluated by independent systems with no shared state, so an edge-unbacked citation could still get credited in Layer 2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the author-guidance AC (or add a rubric-contract test assertion) to require that `docs/writing-a-passing-plan.md` covers at minimum: (1) the citation grammar, (2) the edge-verification requirement (depends_on or blocks edge needed), and (3) that an unbacked citation earns no credit and the missing-symbol finding still stands.
evidence: AC proof is `grep -q "[rebar:"` — a single token match, no content contract
The Grammar section specifies subject + token + ticket-id grammar but no AC requires the guidance to reflect the edge-verification requirement or the advisory-vs-blocking distinction
Authors who add a citation without a depends_on/blocks edge will still get an advisory finding; without guidance covering this, they may not understand why
findings.finding: The author-guidance acceptance criterion verifies only string presence (`grep -q "[rebar:"`) without specifying what the guidance must cover — leaving out whether it explains the edge-verification requirement (citations without a verified depends_on/blocks edge earn no credit), the advisory-only nature, and the grammar's subject field — meaning the doc could pass CI with a single mention that doesn't actually help authors avoid the failure mode.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — 'Author guidance added. PROVE: grep -q "[rebar:" docs/writing-a-passing-plan.md'
scenarios: Author adds `MyClass [rebar:abc-123]` citation but omits the depends_on edge; gets an advisory finding with no context to understand it; the guidance doc passes CI but doesn't explain the requirement
The guidance doc mentions the grammar but not that an unbacked citation earns NO credit (Layer-2 still fails closed), leaving authors with a false sense of security
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC proof to a structured test (e.g., in test_citation_rubric_contract.py) that asserts the guidance doc contains phrases covering the three required concepts, rather than a bare grep for the token.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete N (sample count) and pass-rate threshold for the behavioral eval fixture in the AC, rather than deferring to an implicit precedent lookup.
evidence: 'run over N samples against a predeclared threshold (mirrors the det_operator_attested lexicon-eval self-gating precedent)'
Neither N nor the threshold value is declared anywhere in the plan; 'mirrors the ... precedent' is a reference the executor must independently look up to infer the values.
findings.finding: The behavioral eval AC specifies 'N samples against a predeclared threshold' without stating N or the threshold value, deferring that design choice to the executor.
findings.location: Additional acceptance criteria — RUBRIC BEHAVIORAL EVAL AC
scenarios: An executor setting up the eval fixture under docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/ has no authoritative sample count or pass-rate threshold to configure — they must either guess or go look up the det_operator_attested eval, creating an implicit clarification loop.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'N=30 samples, threshold ≥ 0.80 (matching the det_operator_attested lexicon-eval defaults)' — or cite the specific file/constant that defines those values so the executor has a single authoritative source.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Predeclare the behavioral eval's N (sample count per fixture) and pass-rate threshold in the plan text (e.g., '≥ 4/5 samples correctly credit the verified+covered case and ≥ 4/5 correctly retain the finding for the unbacked case'), matching the specificity of the operator_attested_eval precedent.
evidence: det_operator_attested precedent has: precision >= 70%, flag rate <= 5%, two named regression cases (115b, 8c4f) — see operator_attested_eval.py lines 12-14
Layer-2 eval specifies 'N samples' and 'predeclared threshold' but supplies neither value
The only fixture count given is 'TWO plans' (one positive, one negative)
Proof mechanism is 'eval harness run recorded on the ticket' not a deterministic pytest assertion or committed threshold file
findings.finding: The behavioral eval fixture for Layer-2 (LLM coverage protocol) lacks a predeclared numeric threshold: the plan says it 'mirrors the det_operator_attested lexicon-eval self-gating precedent' and runs 'over N samples against a predeclared threshold', but neither N nor the threshold is stated in the plan. The operator_attested_eval precedent (which this claim mirrors) has concrete thresholds: precision ≥ 70%, flag rate ≤ 5%, and specific regression cases. The Layer-2 eval specifies only 'TWO plans' as fixtures and defers the pass-rate threshold to what is 'recorded on the ticket'. This means the eval is not self-gating in the same deterministic sense as its stated precedent.
findings.location: Additional acceptance criteria: 'RUBRIC BEHAVIORAL EVAL...run over N samples against a predeclared threshold (mirrors the det_operator_attested lexicon-eval self-gating precedent). PROVE: the eval harness run recorded on the ticket.'
scenarios: Implementer runs two samples, gets 1/2 or 2/2 correct, and records any outcome as 'pass' since no threshold is specified in the plan.
A future reviewer cannot reproduce the gate condition without knowing what N and threshold were at the time of original execution.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a concrete threshold line such as: 'N=5 samples per fixture, pass-rate threshold ≥ 4/5 (80%) for both the credit case and the stand-fast case; committed as a pre-registration JSON under docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/ before the eval runs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that caught exceptions in the `resolve_deps` advisory lane are logged (at WARNING level or equivalent) with the ticket-id and exception type so operators can distinguish infrastructure failures from unverified citations.
evidence: 'the reverse-lookup resolve_deps(C)/show_ticket(C) is wrapped so ANY exception... is caught and the citation is treated as UNVERIFIED' — no mention of logging or metrics on the caught exception
No observability specification anywhere in the plan for the new I/O failure path
Existing det_operator_attested precedent cited for structure, but no mention of whether it instruments failures
findings.finding: New failure points (resolve_deps/show_ticket store/network calls, swallowed exceptions in the advisory lane) are not instrumented with any log, metric, or trace. When resolve_deps silently catches an exception and marks a citation as UNVERIFIED, operators have no visibility into whether this is expected behavior or a store outage degrading coverage checks across all in-flight reviews.
findings.location: Round-3 additions — ERROR HANDLING section; det_citation.py (new)
scenarios: A store outage causes every resolve_deps call to throw; all citations are silently treated as unverified; advisory findings fire for every cross-ticket citation; operators see a flood of advisory findings with no indication it's an infrastructure issue rather than authoring errors
A bug in the resolve_deps callable (e.g., wrong field name) silently degrades citation verification for all tickets with no alert
findings.suggested_fix: In det_citation.py's exception handler, emit a structured log entry: `logger.warning('det_citation: resolve_deps failed for cited ticket %s, treating as unverified', cited_id, exc_info=True)`. Add a one-line observability note to the plan's error-handling section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding `test_parse` parametrize case or separate test) specifying what `parse_citations` does with malformed citation tokens (empty id, unclosed bracket, characters outside rebar's id grammar) — e.g., 'silently skips non-conforming tokens' — and confirm no exception escapes.
evidence: AC: '`det_citation.parse_citations(plan_text)` returns `(subject, ticket_id)` per `[rebar:<id>]`. PROVE: `pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review/test_det_citation.py::test_parse`'
Grammar: '`<ticket-id>` matches rebar's id/alias grammar' — no parse-failure behavior specified
ABSENCE: no AC or test covers malformed citation tokens (empty id, unclosed bracket, disallowed characters)
findings.finding: The AC for `test_parse` specifies only the happy-path (well-formed `[rebar:<id>]` tokens), with no coverage of malformed citation tokens — empty id (`[rebar:]`), unclosed bracket (`[rebar:123`), or ids containing characters outside rebar's grammar. The grammar section says ticket_id 'matches rebar's id/alias grammar' but is silent on what parse_citations does with non-conforming tokens, leaving the parser's error contract unspecified and untested.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — `test_parse` AC; Grammar (vocabulary) section
scenarios: A plan author writes `[rebar:]` (empty id) or `[rebar:123` (unclosed) — parse_citations may crash, return a spurious result, or silently skip; callers and the det_floor advisory lane have no specified contract for this case.
A typo in a plan citation causes an unhandled exception that escapes the advisory lane despite the error-handling AC only covering resolve_deps failures.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the grammar section with: 'Malformed tokens (empty id, unclosed bracket, or id characters outside rebar's alias grammar) are silently skipped by parse_citations — they do not produce a citation tuple and do not raise.' Add a parametrized test case to test_parse covering at least `[rebar:]`, `[rebar:123` (unclosed), and `[rebar:a b]` (space in id).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding test case in test_det_citation.py or test_det_floor_wires_citation) asserting that a PlanContext with a Layer-1-VERIFIED `[rebar:<id>]` citation produces NO advisory finding from det_citation.
evidence: 'a PlanContext whose plan has an edge-unbacked [rebar:<id>] yields the advisory citation finding, verdict NOT BLOCK'
No corresponding AC or test case for: 'a PlanContext whose plan has an edge-BACKED [rebar:<id>] yields NO advisory citation finding'.
findings.finding: The wiring AC (test_det_floor_wires_citation) only proves the negative case (edge-unbacked citation emits an advisory finding) — there is no AC proving the positive case: that a Layer-1-VERIFIED citation does NOT emit a spurious advisory finding.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — 'WIRING: det_floor invokes det_citation...' AC
scenarios: An implementation that always emits the advisory finding regardless of edge verification would pass test_det_floor_wires_citation (it does emit a finding for the unbacked case) but would be broken for the core use-case — verified citations wrongly penalized.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'WIRING positive case: a PlanContext whose plan has an edge-BACKED `[rebar:<C>]` (C in P.depends_on or C.blocks(P) confirmed) yields NO advisory citation finding. PROVE: pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review/test_det_citation.py::test_verified_citation_no_advisory_finding'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the criteria-guide AC's proving command to assert the regenerated guide contains citation-protocol content, e.g. append `&& grep -q '\[rebar:' docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md`.
evidence: 'python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry regenerate-criteria-guide && pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review -k "citation or contract"'
The -k filter selects existing citation/contract tests, not a new assertion on the guide's contents; a regeneration that silently omits the new rubric changes would still pass this command.
findings.finding: The criteria-guide AC's proving command does not assert that the regenerated guide actually contains the new citation protocol content — only that the regeneration script exits 0 and unrelated tests pass.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — 'Criteria guide regenerated + plan-review suite green' AC
scenarios: If the registry regeneration reads from rubric .md files but the rubric edits were incomplete, the guide would regenerate with missing content — but the proving command would still exit 0 because no assertion checks the guide output.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the proving command to: `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry regenerate-criteria-guide && grep -q '[rebar:' docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md && pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review -k 'citation or contract'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or design-doc (e.g., `docs/adr/citation-grounding-layers.md`) capturing the two-layer citation architecture: the fail-closed default, both-direction edge verification, anti-bypass guarantee, and the Layer-2 LLM coverage protocol contract — and reference it from AGENTS.md or the plan-review README.
evidence: Files likely touched lists no ADR file
T5e(c): 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update' — absent
The plan itself contains the rationale inline but no durable design doc is produced as an artifact
The two-layer protocol (det_citation advisory lane + LLM coverage judgment with fail-closed default) is a meaningful architectural decision affecting future reviewers extending the grounding pipeline
findings.finding: No ADR or design-doc update is planned for the two-layer citation verification architecture — a novel decision about how deterministic and LLM reviewer layers interact to credit cross-ticket prerequisites, with specific fail-closed semantics, edge-direction rules, and anti-bypass guarantees.
findings.location: Scope section / Files likely touched
scenarios: A future engineer adding a new grounding finder (e.g., E7) has no design-doc to consult for the citation protocol contract, risks re-implementing or bypassing the Layer-1/Layer-2 interaction incorrectly
The anti-bypass guarantee ('crediting requires BOTH a Layer-1-verified edge AND affirmative LLM coverage') is only captured in the ticket narrative, not in a persistent design artifact
findings.suggested_fix: Create docs/adr/citation-grounding-layers.md with sections: Context (problem of cross-ticket symbol grounding), Decision (two-layer advisory-det + LLM-coverage approach), Consequences (fail-closed, never new floor block, anti-bypass), and Reference (det_citation.py contract). Add it to the Files likely touched list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the injected `resolve_deps` callable is necessary: grep shows zero existing DET modules use injectable callables for testability; consider calling `_reads.show_ticket` directly in the advisory lane (with BLE001 try/except as in pass1.py:314-320) and patching it in tests, matching the codebase's established DET-module pattern.
evidence: det_operator_attested.py (the stated pattern model) is a pure stdlib-re leaf — zero I/O, zero injection, tested by calling it directly with fixture strings (tests/unit/test_det_floor_operator_attested.py:29-31).
det_lint.py is similarly a pure leaf with TYPE_CHECKING-guarded PlanContext import and no injectable callables.

Every existing DET module that needs I/O (pass1.py:310-320, orchestrator.py:168-185, attest.py:737-784) calls `_reads.show_ticket` directly inside a BLE001 try/except — there is no injectable callable pattern in this codebase's DET or plan-review layer.
Grep for 'injected' in plan_review/ shows the word used only in docstring prose (drift_floor, xcheck, pass1, sizing), never as a DI seam for a callable dependency.
The plan itself notes det_citation will be 'a pure leaf (stdlib re only)' in the grounding section — but then proposes I/O via the injected callable, contradicting the 'pure leaf' characterization.
findings.finding: The injected `resolve_deps` callable is a novel DI pattern with zero precedent in the DET module family; it is inconsistent with how the codebase achieves DET-module testability.
findings.location: Round-3 additions: 'The `resolve_deps` callable is injected (not a hard `_reads` import inside det_citation) purely so the edge-check is unit-testable with a fake resolver.'
scenarios: Implementer must decide the callable's signature, error contract, and injection point in det_floor — none of which are fully specified — adding surface area with no existing precedent to follow.
Future DET modules cargo-cult the injectable seam, proliferating a pattern that isn't the codebase's actual convention.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `workflow_ops.py` to Files likely touched (or explicitly note it requires no change) and confirm that the `resolve_deps` callable injected by det_floor correctly propagates `ctx.tickets_root` when doing the `_reads.show_ticket` reverse lookup.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:36: `from rebar.llm.plan_review.det_operator_attested import ...`
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:167: `det_results = det_floor.run_det_floor(pctx)`
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:180: `det_results = det_floor.run_det_floor(pctx)`

workflow_ops.py supplies pctx to run_det_floor, which is where det_citation will be invoked; the resolve_deps callable needs access to tickets_root, which lives on pctx but the injection point is det_floor internal.
findings.finding: `workflow_ops.py` directly imports `det_operator_attested` from the plan_review package (`from rebar.llm.plan_review.det_operator_attested import ...`) and consumes `det_advisory_findings` from `det_floor`. It is a caller OUTSIDE `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` that interacts with the advisory-lane wiring this plan changes, yet it is not listed in 'Files likely touched'. If `det_citation` advisory findings need special handling (e.g. a new coverage key) or if `workflow_ops` needs to pass a `tickets_root`-aware `resolve_deps` to the floor, `workflow_ops.py` is the call site that would need updating.
findings.location: Files likely touched — omission of workflow_ops.py
scenarios: The `resolve_deps` callable injected into det_citation needs `tickets_root` from `pctx` to read prerequisite tickets correctly in the attested-gate path (where the ticket store is on a separate branch). If det_floor constructs resolve_deps internally without propagating tickets_root, reverse lookups will spuriously fail in CI attestation runs.
findings.suggested_fix: In det_floor's advisory-lane wiring, construct resolve_deps as `lambda cid: _reads.show_ticket(cid, repo_root=ctx.tickets_root).get('deps', [])` so it uses the same tickets_root that the rest of the floor uses.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly whether det_citation is wired as (a) a new DET_CHECKS entry with its own P-id, or (b) called from within an existing p-check (e.g., p6_ac_quality), and add a rationale sentence explaining why that integration point was chosen over the alternative.
evidence: det_operator_attested is surfaced via p6_ac_quality() at det_floor.py:452-489, NOT as a DET_CHECKS entry — it has no own P-id
DET_CHECKS tuple at det_floor.py:651-661 currently contains P1-P9; a new DET_CHECKS entry would extend the frozen floor
The plan states det_floor is '746/800 LOC (near the hard module-size cap)' as rationale for extraction — but a new DET_CHECKS entry still requires adding wiring lines in det_floor.py
AC test_det_floor_wires_citation requires 'det_floor invokes det_citation in its advisory lane' — specifies behavior but not the integration point
findings.finding: The plan says det_citation is 'wired into det_floor's advisory lane' but does not specify whether this means (a) a new P10-style entry added to the DET_CHECKS tuple (giving det_citation its own DetResult/P-id and independent coverage slot) or (b) called from inside an existing P-check (as det_operator_attested is called from p6_ac_quality). These are architecturally distinct: option (a) adds to the frozen DET_CHECKS tuple and increases det_floor's LOC (counter to the stated module-size rationale); option (b) bundles citation findings under another P-check's coverage id, obscuring them. The analog (det_operator_attested) uses option (b), but the plan doesn't commit to either, leaving the implementer to guess.
findings.location: Layer 1 — deterministic, ADVISORY section: 'wired into det_floor's advisory lane'; Files likely touched: det_floor.py (advisory-lane wiring)
scenarios: Implementer adds det_citation as a new DET_CHECKS function, exceeding the module-size cap the plan explicitly cites as a constraint

Implementer bundles it into p6_ac_quality (like det_operator_attested), causing citation findings to appear under the P6 criterion id rather than a dedicated id, making them harder to query/filter in coverage
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the det_operator_attested pattern: call det_citation from within p6_ac_quality (or a new advisory p-check called from run_det_floor outside DET_CHECKS via the dynamic project-DET phase) so det_floor.py gains minimal lines. State this explicitly so the implementer does not invent a new P10 entry.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
